title: Sum of divisors of N
description: Given N, compute sum of all positive divisors

approaches.name: Brute Force O(N)
approaches.summary: Iterate from 1 to N, check if each integer divides N, and sum the divisors. This is straightforward but inefficient for large N.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Divisors Using Brute Force O(N)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to compute sum of all positive divisors of N by iterating from 1 to N.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the sum of divisors is based on the fundamental definition of divisibility. A divisor of N is any positive integer i such that N divided by i leaves no remainder (N % i == 0). The intuition is straightforward: we systematically check every integer from 1 up to N itself to see if it divides N evenly. For each divisor found, we add it to a running total. This method is exhaustive—it guarantees we find all divisors because we examine every possible candidate. The algorithm begins by initializing a sum variable to 0. Then, we loop through i from 1 to N inclusive. In each iteration, we check if N modulo i equals 0. If true, i is a divisor, so we add i to the sum. After the loop completes, the sum contains the total of all positive divisors. The time complexity is O(N) because we perform a constant-time check for each of N iterations. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables regardless of N. This approach is simple to understand and implement, making it excellent for learning, but it becomes inefficient for large N (e.g., N > 10^6) due to linear time growth. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop range (e.g., stopping at N-1 instead of N, which would miss N itself as a divisor), incorrect modulo operation, or not initializing the sum properly. To avoid these, ensure the loop includes N and use clear variable names. This connects directly to the problem statement as it provides a complete, working solution using the brute force method.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisor Checking and Accumulation Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that iterates from 1 to N, checks divisibility, and sums valid divisors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach, responsible for identifying and summing divisors through iteration. The intuition is to traverse all possible divisor candidates systematically. We use a for-loop that runs from 1 to N, inclusive, because divisors can range from 1 (the smallest positive divisor) to N itself (since N divided by N equals 1). In each iteration, we check if N is divisible by the current number i using the modulo operator (%). If N % i == 0, it means i divides N without remainder, so i is a divisor. We then add i to a running sum variable. This process accumulates all divisors into a single total. The step-by-step algorithm: initialize sum to 0; for i from 1 to N: if N % i == 0, then sum = sum + i; after the loop, return sum. Time complexity is O(N) due to the loop iterating N times, with each iteration doing constant-time operations (modulo check and addition). Space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used. This sub-routine connects to the full problem (L0) by providing the core iterative logic; the full problem essentially wraps this with input handling and output. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., starting at 0, which would cause division by zero errors, or ending at N-1, missing N as a divisor), using integer division instead of modulo for the check, or not updating the sum correctly. To avoid these, always start the loop at 1, use <= for the upper bound, and double-check the modulo operation. Real code pattern: sum = 0; for i in range(1, N+1): if N % i == 0: sum += i; return sum.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisibility Check Using Modulo Operator
ladders.desc: Determine if one number divides another evenly using the modulo operation.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is checking divisibility, which relies on the modulo operator. The intuition is that if a number i divides N, then the remainder when N is divided by i should be zero. In arithmetic, for integers N and i (with i > 0), we say i divides N if there exists an integer k such that N = i * k. The modulo operator (%) computes the remainder of the division N / i. If N % i == 0, it means the division is exact with no remainder, confirming i is a divisor. This check is fundamental because it allows us to test each candidate i efficiently without performing full division. The step-by-step process: given N and i, compute remainder = N % i; if remainder == 0, then i is a divisor; otherwise, it is not. Time complexity is O(1) per check, as modulo is a constant-time operation. Space complexity is O(1). This logic feeds into the key sub-routine (L1) by being the condition inside the loop; without it, we couldn't identify which numbers to sum. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division (e.g., using N // i == 0, which is incorrect because integer division gives the quotient, not the remainder), or not handling edge cases like i = 0 (which would cause an error, but in our context, i starts from 1). To avoid errors, always use % for remainder checks and ensure i is positive. Real code pattern: if N % i == 0: # i is a divisor.

examples.trace: Compute 10 % 2 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 2 divides 10 evenly. Return true.
Compute 10 % 3 = 1. Since remainder is 1 (not 0), 3 does not divide 10 evenly. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through a Range of Numbers
ladders.desc: Loop from a start value to an end value, inclusive, to process each number in sequence.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a loop that iterates over a consecutive range of integers, which is essential for testing each potential divisor. The intuition is to systematically cover all numbers from 1 to N without skipping any, ensuring we check every possible divisor. In programming, this is typically done with a for-loop that uses a counter variable (e.g., i) that increments from the start value to the end value. For the divisor sum problem, we need to include both endpoints: start at 1 (since 1 is always a divisor) and end at N (since N is always a divisor). The step-by-step algorithm: initialize i = start; while i <= end: perform some operation on i; then increment i by 1. In pseudocode, this is often written as for i in range(start, end+1). Time complexity depends on the number of iterations; for a range of size N, it's O(N). Space complexity is O(1) if only the counter is used. This block connects to the core logic (L2) by providing the iteration mechanism; we apply the divisibility check to each i in the range. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, such as using i < end instead of i <= end (which would exclude the end value), or starting at 0 (which is not a positive divisor). To avoid these, carefully set loop bounds and test with small values. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, N+1): # process i.

examples.trace: Initialize i=1. Process i=1. Increment i to 2. Process i=2. Increment i to 3. Process i=3. Increment i to 4. Process i=4. Increment i to 5. Process i=5. Stop as i=6 > end=5.
Initialize i=3. Process i=3. Increment i to 4. Process i=4. Increment i to 5. Process i=5. Increment i to 6. Process i=6. Stop as i=7 > end=6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation is a fundamental arithmetic operation that calculates the remainder of division between two integers. The intuition is that when we divide a number N by another number i, we get a quotient and a remainder; the modulo gives us the remainder. Formally, for integers N and i (with i != 0), N % i returns the remainder r such that 0 <= r < |i| and N = i * q + r for some integer q. In the context of divisor checking, if N % i == 0, it means the remainder is zero, so i divides N exactly. This operation is crucial because it allows us to test divisibility without performing full division or dealing with fractions. The step-by-step process: given N and i, perform integer division to find how many times i fits into N, then compute remainder = N - (i * (N // i)). In practice, programming languages provide the % operator to do this directly. Time complexity is O(1), as it's a basic arithmetic operation. Space complexity is O(1). This operation feeds into the building block (L3) as it's used within the loop to check each number. Common mistakes include using modulo with i = 0 (which causes division by zero error), misunderstanding that modulo can return negative remainders in some languages (but in Python-style, it's always non-negative), or confusing it with division. To avoid errors, ensure i is positive and use % correctly. Real code pattern: remainder = N % i.

examples.trace: Divide 10 by 3: quotient = 3, remainder = 10 - (3*3) = 10 - 9 = 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
Divide 15 by 5: quotient = 3, remainder = 15 - (5*3) = 15 - 15 = 0. So 15 % 5 = 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Remainders
ladders.desc: Understand how division works with integers, producing a quotient and remainder.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basic arithmetic of integer division, which is prerequisite for understanding the modulo operation. The intuition is that when we divide one whole number by another, we can think of it as splitting the dividend into equal parts of size divisor, resulting in a quotient (how many full parts) and a remainder (what's left over). For example, dividing 10 by 3: 3 fits into 10 three times (since 3*3=9), with 1 left over. So, quotient = 3, remainder = 1. Formally, for integers N (dividend) and i (divisor, i > 0), there exist unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that N = i * q + r, where 0 <= r < i. This relationship is key because it defines divisibility: if r = 0, then i divides N exactly. In programming, we use integer division (//) to get q and modulo (%) to get r. This concept is essential for the brute force approach because checking divisibility relies on computing the remainder. Step-by-step: to divide N by i, repeatedly subtract i from N until N < i; the number of subtractions is q, and the final N is r. Time complexity for manual computation is O(q), but in practice, it's O(1) with built-in operations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the basic operation (L4) as it explains the theory behind modulo. Common mistakes include confusing integer division with floating-point division, or not understanding that remainder must be less than the divisor. To avoid these, practice with small numbers and visualize the splitting process. Real code pattern: q = N // i; r = N % i.

examples.trace: Divide 13 by 4: 4 fits into 13 three times (4*3=12), remainder = 13 - 12 = 1. So quotient = 3, remainder = 1.
Divide 20 by 5: 5 fits into 20 four times (5*4=20), remainder = 20 - 20 = 0. So quotient = 4, remainder = 0.

approaches.name: Optimized Iterative O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Iterate up to sqrt(N), and for each divisor i, add both i and N/i if i divides N, handling perfect squares carefully. This reduces iterations significantly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Divisors Using Optimized Iterative Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution to compute sum of all positive divisors of N using O(sqrt(N)) iteration.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the sum of all positive divisors of a given integer N. The naive approach would iterate through all numbers from 1 to N, checking divisibility, resulting in O(N) time complexity. However, we can optimize significantly by observing that divisors come in pairs (i, N/i). For any divisor i, N/i is also a divisor unless i = sqrt(N). By iterating only up to sqrt(N), we can collect both divisors in each step, reducing iterations to O(sqrt(N)). The intuition is rooted in symmetry: if i divides N, then N/i is an integer and also divides N. We must handle perfect squares carefully to avoid double-counting sqrt(N). The algorithm initializes sum = 0, then loops i from 1 to sqrt(N). For each i, if N % i == 0, we add i to sum. If i != N/i, we also add N/i. This ensures all divisors are included exactly once. Time complexity is O(sqrt(N)) due to the loop, and space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is efficient for large N up to around 10^12. Common mistakes include double-counting sqrt(N) for perfect squares, integer overflow when N is large, and missing the case where i = 1. To avoid these, explicitly check i != N/i and use appropriate data types. This solution directly applies the mathematical insight of divisor pairs, making it both elegant and practical.

examples.trace:
N = 12, sqrt(N) ≈ 3.46, so iterate i = 1 to 3.
Step 1: i = 1, 12 % 1 == 0 → add i = 1, N/i = 12 (since 1 != 12) → add 12. Sum = 1 + 12 = 13.
Step 2: i = 2, 12 % 2 == 0 → add i = 2, N/i = 6 (since 2 != 6) → add 6. Sum = 13 + 2 + 6 = 21.
Step 3: i = 3, 12 % 3 == 0 → add i = 3, N/i = 4 (since 3 != 4) → add 4. Sum = 21 + 3 + 4 = 28.
Loop ends. Output sum = 28.

N = 16, sqrt(N) = 4, so iterate i = 1 to 4.
Step 1: i = 1, 16 % 1 == 0 → add i = 1, N/i = 16 (since 1 != 16) → add 16. Sum = 1 + 16 = 17.
Step 2: i = 2, 16 % 2 == 0 → add i = 2, N/i = 8 (since 2 != 8) → add 8. Sum = 17 + 2 + 8 = 27.
Step 3: i = 3, 16 % 3 != 0 → skip.
Step 4: i = 4, 16 % 4 == 0 → add i = 4, N/i = 4 (since 4 == 4, perfect square) → only add 4 once. Sum = 27 + 4 = 31.
Loop ends. Output sum = 31.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterating Up to sqrt(N) and Handling Divisor Pairs
ladders.desc: The core loop that iterates to sqrt(N) and adds divisor pairs to compute the sum.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the optimized approach. It involves a loop from 1 to sqrt(N), where for each i, we check if i divides N (i.e., N % i == 0). If true, we add i to the sum. Then, to include the paired divisor, we check if i != N/i. If they are different, we add N/i as well. This ensures all divisors are accounted for without duplication. The intuition is based on the mathematical property that divisors are symmetric around sqrt(N). For example, for N=12, divisors are (1,12), (2,6), (3,4). By iterating i=1 to 3, we capture all pairs. The key insight is that every divisor less than sqrt(N) has a corresponding divisor greater than sqrt(N), except when i = sqrt(N) in perfect squares. The algorithm uses integer arithmetic to compute sqrt(N) via floor(sqrt(N)) to avoid floating-point issues. Time complexity is O(sqrt(N)) for the loop, and space is O(1). This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by providing the mechanism to compute the sum efficiently. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt which can cause precision errors, or forgetting to handle the perfect square case where i = N/i. To avoid, use integer sqrt methods and explicitly check i != N/i. This routine is a classic pattern in divisor-related problems.

examples.input: N=20, compute sum using this sub-routine
N=9, compute sum using this sub-routine
examples.trace:
N = 9, sqrt(N) = 3, so iterate i = 1 to 3.
Step 1: i = 1, 9 % 1 == 0 → add 1, N/i = 9 (1 != 9) → add 9. Sum = 10.
Step 2: i = 2, 9 % 2 != 0 → skip.
Step 3: i = 3, 9 % 3 == 0 → add 3, N/i = 3 (3 == 3, perfect square) → only add 3 once. Sum = 10 + 3 = 13.
Output sum = 13.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding Divisor Pairs and Symmetry Around sqrt(N)
ladders.desc: The mathematical insight that divisors come in pairs, reducing the search space to sqrt(N).
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central mathematical concept: for any positive integer N, if i is a divisor of N, then N/i is also a divisor. This creates pairs (i, N/i). The key observation is that one divisor in each pair is ≤ sqrt(N) and the other is ≥ sqrt(N). Therefore, by iterating only up to sqrt(N), we can find all divisors by capturing both i and N/i for each i that divides N. This symmetry halves the search space, leading to O(sqrt(N)) efficiency. The intuition is geometric: if you think of divisors as factors, they balance around the square root. For example, with N=12, sqrt(12)≈3.46, divisors are 1,2,3,4,6,12. Pairs are (1,12), (2,6), (3,4). By checking i=1,2,3, we get all six divisors. The algorithm leverages this by checking divisibility for i up to sqrt(N) and adding both i and N/i when i divides N. Special care is needed for perfect squares where i = sqrt(N), so N/i = i, and we add it only once to avoid double-counting. Time analysis: iterating to sqrt(N) gives O(sqrt(N)) steps. Space is O(1). This logic feeds into the sub-routine by providing the rationale for the loop structure. Common mistakes include not understanding why sqrt(N) is the boundary, or mishandling the perfect square case. To avoid, visualize divisor pairs and test with examples. This concept is fundamental in number theory and optimization problems.

examples.trace:
N = 28, sqrt(N) ≈ 5.29. List divisors: 1,2,4,7,14,28.
Find pairs where product equals N:
1 * 28 = 28 → pair (1,28)
2 * 14 = 28 → pair (2,14)
4 * 7 = 28 → pair (4,7)
Note: 4 ≤ sqrt(28), 7 ≥ sqrt(28). All divisors covered.

N = 25, sqrt(N) = 5. List divisors: 1,5,25.
Find pairs:
1 * 25 = 25 → pair (1,25)
5 * 5 = 25 → pair (5,5) (perfect square, same divisor)
Note: 5 = sqrt(25), so it appears once in the pair.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Divisibility and Computing Integer Square Root
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: determining if a number divides another, and finding floor(sqrt(N)).
ladders.explanation: This level covers two essential operations needed for the optimized approach. First, checking divisibility: given two integers a and b, we say a divides b if b % a == 0, where % is the modulo operator returning the remainder. This is a basic arithmetic operation with O(1) time. The intuition is that if division results in no remainder, a is a divisor. Second, computing the integer square root: we need floor(sqrt(N)), i.e., the largest integer i such that i*i ≤ N. This can be done using a loop from 1 upwards until i*i > N, or using built-in functions like int(math.sqrt(N)) in Python, but care must be taken to avoid floating-point inaccuracies. A safe method is to use integer arithmetic with while i*i <= N. Time complexity for this computation is O(sqrt(N)) if done naively, but it's often optimized or precomputed. In the full algorithm, we use this sqrt to limit the loop. These operations feed into the core logic by enabling the iteration and divisibility checks. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt which might give slightly off values for large N, or incorrect modulo checks. To avoid, use integer-based sqrt methods and ensure modulo is applied correctly. This building block is crucial for many algorithmic problems involving factors or optimization.

examples.input: Check divisibility for N=15, i=3 and i=4
examples.trace:
For i=3: Compute 15 % 3 = 0 → remainder 0, so 3 divides 15.
For i=4: Compute 15 % 4 = 3 → remainder 3 (non-zero), so 4 does not divide 15.

We need largest integer i such that i*i ≤ 20.
Try i=1: 1*1=1 ≤ 20 → yes.
i=2: 4 ≤ 20 → yes.
i=3: 9 ≤ 20 → yes.
i=4: 16 ≤ 20 → yes.
i=5: 25 > 20 → no.
Thus, floor(sqrt(20)) = 4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping and Conditional Statements
ladders.desc: Using loops to iterate and conditionals to make decisions in an algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces basic programming constructs: loops and conditional statements, which are foundational for implementing algorithms. A loop allows repeating a block of code multiple times. In this context, we use a for-loop to iterate i from 1 to sqrt(N). Conditional statements (if-else) are used to check conditions, such as whether i divides N (N % i == 0). The intuition is that algorithms often require repetitive actions and decisions based on data. For example, to sum divisors, we loop through possible divisors and add them only if they divide N. The step-by-step process involves initializing a sum variable, setting up the loop with a counter i, and inside the loop, using an if statement to test divisibility. If true, update the sum. This operation has time complexity proportional to the number of iterations and O(1) space. It feeds into the building block by providing the structure to implement divisibility checks and sqrt computation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, incorrect condition checks, or not initializing variables. To avoid, carefully define loop limits and test with small examples. This is a fundamental skill in programming and algorithm design.

examples.input: Loop i from 1 to 5 and print if i is even
examples.trace:
Initialize i=1.
Step 1: i=1, check if even (1%2==0) → false, skip.
Step 2: i=2, check if even → true, print 2.
Step 3: i=3, check if even → false, skip.
Step 4: i=4, check if even → true, print 4.
Step 5: i=5, check if even → false, skip.
Loop ends. Output: 2, 4.

examples.input: Sum numbers from 1 to 3 using a loop
examples.trace:
Initialize sum=0.
Step 1: i=1, add to sum: sum = 0+1=1.
Step 2: i=2, add to sum: sum = 1+2=3.
Step 3: i=3, add to sum: sum = 3+3=6.
Loop ends. Output sum=6.

testCases.input: Loop i=1 to 4, print if i>2
Loop i=1 to 3, print 'yes' if i==2 else 'no'
Loop i=1 to 2, print i twice
Check if numbers 1,2,3 are less than 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers, Division, and Remainders
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic concepts: what integers are, how division works, and the meaning of remainder.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the absolute basics needed to approach the problem. Integers are whole numbers (e.g., 1, 2, 3) including zero and negatives, but here we focus on positive integers. Division is the operation of splitting a number into equal parts. When dividing integer a by integer b (b ≠ 0), we get a quotient and a remainder. For example, 7 ÷ 3 gives quotient 2 and remainder 1, because 3*2 + 1 = 7. The remainder is what's left after dividing as much as possible. In programming, we use the modulo operator (%) to get the remainder. If remainder is 0, we say b divides a evenly, meaning a is a multiple of b. This is key for checking divisors. The intuition is that divisors are numbers that divide N with no remainder. For instance, divisors of 6 are 1,2,3,6 because 6%1=0, 6%2=0, etc. This foundation feeds into basic operations by providing the mathematical basis for conditionals in loops. Common mistakes include confusing quotient and remainder, or dividing by zero. To avoid, practice with simple examples and understand that division by zero is undefined. This concept is prerequisite for any problem involving factors or multiples.

examples.input: Divide 10 by 3, find quotient and remainder
examples.trace: 10 ÷ 3: 3 fits into 10 three times (3*3=9), with 1 left over (10-9=1). So quotient=3, remainder=1. Check: 3*3 + 1 = 10.
Compute 12 % 4: 12 ÷ 4 = 3 exactly, remainder=0. Since remainder is 0, 4 divides 12 evenly.

approaches.name: Prime Factorization Formula O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Factorize N into prime powers, then compute the sum using the formula based on geometric series for each prime factor. This is efficient and mathematically elegant.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Divisors Using Prime Factorization Formula
ladders.desc: Complete solution: factorize N into prime powers, then compute divisor sum using geometric series formula for each factor.
ladders.explanation:
This approach leverages the mathematical property that the sum of divisors can be computed from the prime factorization of N. The intuition is that divisors are formed by combining different powers of prime factors. If N = p₁^a₁ × p₂^a₂ × ... × pₖ^aₖ, then the sum of divisors σ(N) = ∏(p_i^(a_i+1) - 1)/(p_i - 1). This works because for each prime factor p with exponent a, the divisors contributed by that prime are p⁰, p¹, ..., p^a, whose sum is a geometric series equal to (p^(a+1)-1)/(p-1). The total sum multiplies these contributions because divisors combine independently across different primes.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize result = 1
2. For each prime factor p found (starting from 2):
   a. Count exponent a = 0
   b. While N % p == 0: N = N/p, a = a+1
   c. If a > 0: result *= (p^(a+1) - 1)/(p - 1)
3. If N > 1 (remaining prime): result *= (N^2 - 1)/(N - 1) = N+1
4. Return result

Time complexity: O(√N) since we check divisors up to √N. Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This uses the prime factorization subroutine from L1 as its core component.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle the remaining prime when N>1 after loop; integer overflow when computing p^(a+1); not using integer division properly.

Real code pattern:
def sum_of_divisors(N):
    result = 1
    p = 2
    while p * p <= N:
        if N % p == 0:
            a = 0
            while N % p == 0:
                N //= p
                a += 1
            result *= (p**(a+1) - 1) // (p - 1)
        p += 1 if p == 2 else 2  # Skip even numbers after 2
    if N > 1:
        result *= (N**2 - 1) // (N - 1)  # = N+1
    return result

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization of N
ladders.desc: Factorize N into prime powers: find all prime factors with their exponents.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine decomposes N into its prime factors, which is essential for computing the divisor sum. The intuition is that every integer >1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime powers (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). We find factors by trial division up to √N.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize empty list/dict for factors
2. For p from 2 to √N:
   a. If N%p==0:
      i. Count exponent a=0
      ii. While N%p==0: N=N/p, a=a+1
      iii. Record factor p with exponent a
3. If N>1: record N as prime factor with exponent 1
4. Return factorization

Time: O(√N) worst-case (when N is prime). Space: O(k) where k is number of distinct prime factors.

Connection to L0: This provides the factorization needed for the divisor sum formula.

Common mistakes: Not handling remaining N>1; inefficiently checking all numbers instead of just primes; not optimizing to check only up to √N.

Real code pattern:
def prime_factors(N):
    factors = {}
    p = 2
    while p * p <= N:
        if N % p == 0:
            exp = 0
            while N % p == 0:
                N //= p
                exp += 1
            factors[p] = exp
        p += 1 if p == 2 else 2
    if N > 1:
        factors[N] = 1
    return factors

ladders.title: Core Logic — Geometric Series Formula for Prime Power Divisor Sum
ladders.desc: Compute sum of divisors contributed by a single prime power: σ(p^a) = (p^(a+1)-1)/(p-1).
ladders.explanation:
This is the mathematical core that makes the approach efficient. For a prime p raised to power a, the divisors contributed are: p⁰, p¹, p², ..., p^a. These form a geometric series with first term 1, common ratio p, and a+1 terms. The sum of geometric series is (first_term×(ratio^(terms)-1))/(ratio-1) = (1×(p^(a+1)-1))/(p-1).

Why this works: Every divisor of p^a must be p^k for some k from 0 to a. Summing all these gives the total contribution from this prime factor.

Algorithm:
Input: prime p, exponent a
Return: (p^(a+1) - 1) // (p - 1)

Time: O(log a) for exponentiation. Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This formula is applied to each prime factor from the factorization.

Common mistakes: Using floating point division instead of integer; overflow for large p^a; misunderstanding the exponent (should be a+1 not a).

Real code pattern:
def prime_power_divisor_sum(p, a):
    # Compute p^(a+1) efficiently
    numerator = pow(p, a+1) - 1
    denominator = p - 1
    return numerator // denominator

examples.trace:
p=2, a=2
p^(a+1) = 2^(3) = 8
Numerator = 8-1 = 7
Denominator = 2-1 = 1
Result = 7//1 = 7
Divisors of 2^2=4: 1,2,4 → sum=7

p=3, a=1
p^(a+1) = 3^(2) = 9
Numerator = 9-1 = 8
Denominator = 3-1 = 2
Result = 8//2 = 4
Divisors of 3^1=3: 1,3 → sum=4

ladders.title: Building Block — Efficient Exponentiation (Power Calculation)
ladders.desc: Compute p^exp efficiently using fast exponentiation.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept calculates p raised to a power efficiently, which is needed for the geometric series formula. Naive multiplication takes O(exp) time, but we can do O(log exp) using exponentiation by squaring.

Intuition: Instead of multiplying p exp times, we use the binary representation of exp. For example, p^13 = p^8 × p^4 × p^1 because 13 in binary is 1101 (8+4+1).

Algorithm (iterative):
1. Initialize result = 1
2. While exp > 0:
   a. If exp is odd: result = result × p
   b. p = p × p
   c. exp = exp // 2
3. Return result

Time: O(log exp). Space: O(1).

Connection to L2: This efficiently computes p^(a+1) in the geometric series formula.

Common mistakes: Not handling exp=0 correctly; integer overflow; confusing the update order.

Real code pattern:
def power(p, exp):
    result = 1
    while exp > 0:
        if exp % 2 == 1:
            result *= p
        p *= p
        exp //= 2
    return result

examples.trace:
p=2, exp=3
result=1
exp=3>0, odd: result=1*2=2, p=2*2=4, exp=3//2=1
exp=1>0, odd: result=2*4=8, p=4*4=16, exp=1//2=0
exp=0, stop
Return 8

p=3, exp=4
result=1
exp=4>0, even: result=1, p=3*3=9, exp=4//2=2
exp=2>0, even: result=1, p=9*9=81, exp=2//2=1
exp=1>0, odd: result=1*81=81, p=81*81=6561, exp=1//2=0
Return 81

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trial Division to Check Divisibility
ladders.desc: Check if a number divides another evenly and extract factors.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation tests whether p divides N (N % p == 0) and repeatedly divides N by p while it's divisible. This is the workhorse of prime factorization.

Intuition: If p divides N, then N = p × k for some integer k. By repeatedly dividing, we extract all powers of p from N.

Algorithm:
1. While N % p == 0:
   a. N = N / p
   b. Increment count of divisions
2. Return updated N and count

Time: O(log_p N) worst-case. Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: This is used within the factorization loop to extract prime factors.

Common mistakes: Not using integer division; infinite loop if not updating N; off-by-one in count.

Real code pattern:
def extract_factor(N, p):
    count = 0
    while N % p == 0:
        N //= p
        count += 1
    return N, count

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Remainder (Modulo)
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic operations: division (//) and modulo (%) with integers.
ladders.explanation:
This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding integer division and remainder. For integers a and b (b≠0), a // b gives the quotient (how many times b fits into a), and a % b gives the remainder. These operations are fundamental to checking divisibility: if a % b == 0, then b divides a evenly.

Intuition: When we divide integers, we get a whole number quotient and possibly a remainder. The modulo operation tells us what's left over.

Examples:
10 // 3 = 3 (3 fits into 10 three times)
10 % 3 = 1 (remainder 1)
If remainder is 0, the division is exact.

Time: O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space: O(1).

Connection to L4: These operations are used to check N % p == 0 and perform N //= p.

Common mistakes: Confusing / (float division) with // (integer division); not handling division by zero; misunderstanding negative remainders.

Real code pattern:
# Check divisibility
if N % p == 0:
    # p divides N evenly
    N = N // p  # Integer division

examples.trace:
10 divided by 3:
3 fits into 10 three times (3×3=9)
Remainder = 10-9 = 1
So 10 // 3 = 3, 10 % 3 = 1

15 divided by 5:
5 fits into 15 three times exactly (3×5=15)
Remainder = 15-15 = 0
So 15 // 5 = 3, 15 % 5 = 0 (5 divides 15)
